output:
## Causal Analysis

**Claim:** Intervening to make everyone like spicy food → higher overall probability of high salary.

**Causal structure (given):**

```
Skill (unobserved)
  ├──→ Occupation
  └──→ Salary
Spicy Food
  ├──→ Occupation ──→ Salary
  └──→ Salary (direct)
```

**Step 1 — Check for confounding between Spicy Food and Salary.**

Spicy Food has **no parents** in this graph. Skill (unobserved) affects Occupation and Salary but does **not** cause Spicy Food. So there is no backdoor path from Spicy Food to Salary. No confounding exists.

This means: **P(Salary = high | do(Spicy = 1)) = P(Salary = high | Spicy = 1) = 0.40**

**Step 2 — Compute current overall probability of high salary.**

P(high salary) = P(Spicy=1) · P(high salary | Spicy=1) + P(Spicy=0) · P(high salary | Spicy=0)
= 0.83 × 0.40 + 0.17 × 0.19
= 0.332 + 0.0323
= **0.3643**

**Step 3 — Compare.**

| Scenario | P(high salary) |
|---|---|
| Current world | 36.43% |
| Intervention (everyone likes spicy food) | 40.00% |

**Yes**, the intervention would increase the overall likelihood of a high salary, from approximately 36.4% to 40.0%.

The increase is straightforward here because (a) there is no confounding to worry about, and (b) P(high salary | Spicy=1) = 40% is already higher than the population-weighted average of 36.4%, so forcing everyone into the higher-probability group raises the overall rate.
